entry.title: Brazil Time Bank: 1x / 1.7x Overtime with 1:1 Leave Deduction
entry.content:
Hi SF Time Experts,

I'm looking for some guidance on how others have implemented a Brazil overtime/shortime (working time) bank requirement in SuccessFactors Time Tracking. It is a legal requirement.

The business requirement is as follows:

  • Overtime worked during the week and on Saturdays is paid at the normal hourly rate (1x).
  • Overtime worked on Sundays and public holidays is paid at 1.7x the normal hourly rate.
  • Employees can take time off against their banked overtime or short time can deduct against this. When they do, the deduction is always made on a 1:1 basis, regardless of the rate at which the overtime was earned. The leave should deduct from the 1x hours first and then 1.7x hours. For example, if an employee takes one hour of time off, one hour is deducted from their overtime bank, irrespective of whether that hour was earned at 1x or 1.7x.
  • The overtime bank is settled twice per year (March settlement positive balances and September settlement positive and negative balances). At settlement, the hours are transferred to Payroll in two separate wage types - one containing the underlying hours earned at the normal rate and another containing the underlying hours earned at the 1.7x rate. The hours are transferred without any valuation, and Payroll applies the appropriate payment rate.

I'm interested in understanding how others have designed this in  SuccessFactors Time Tracking.

In particular:

  • How did you model the different overtime rates while ensuring leave always deducts on a 1:1 basis in the correct order 1x first and then 1.7x.
  • How did you manage the transfer of the underlying hours to Payroll while preserving the distinction of the correct rate for settlement?
  • Did you use Working Time Accounts (how did you accumuluate into 2 different accounts), Time Collectors (how did you manage the deductions and payouts), custom logic or another approach? Or were not able implement this at all. 

I'd really appreciate hearing how others have approached this requirement or any lessons learned from similar Brazil implementations.

Thanks in advance!

Kind regards,
Manon

@Volker_Ruof . @PriyankaAgarwal or any other experts I would appreciate your insights, please.  

SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking

entry.title: SAP's EU Commitment Decision: A Win for On-Premise Customer Choice
entry.content:
Introduction

In a significant move for the SAP ecosystem, SAP and the European Commission concluded a competition investigation on July 9, 2026, regarding SAP's on-premise maintenance and support practices. This is great news for customers who manage complex on-premise environments and want more flexibility in their IT strategies.

What Happened?

The European Commission had been investigating certain aspects of SAP's on-premise maintenance and support policies. After a constructive dialogue, SAP offered commitments that the Commission accepted, closing the matter.

Key Takeaways for SAP Customers

1. Greater Transparency – SAP will make its maintenance policies more transparent, so customers know exactly what they're paying for and what options they have.
2. More Flexibility – Targeted flexibility for exceptional "shelfware" situations (software that's licensed but unused), giving customers relief where it matters most.
3. Freedom to Choose Support Providers – Customers can now choose different ERP support providers, reducing vendor lock-in.
4. Consistent Execution – Improved guidance, training, and independent oversight to ensure policies are applied consistently worldwide.
5. Cloud Not Affected – This decision relates solely to on-premise maintenance. SAP's cloud offerings remain unchanged.

Why This Matters

For organizations still running SAP on-premise (which is a large majority) this means:

- More predictable costs
- Ability to modernize toward cloud/AI at your own pace
- Less pressure to migrate purely due to support pricing

As SAP itself stated: "The added clarity and flexibility support customers as they modernize toward an AI-enabled autonomous enterprise at their own pace."

My Thoughts

This is a positive step for the entire SAP community. It shows SAP is listening to customer concerns and willing to adapt. Whether you're planning an S/4HANA Cloud migration or maintaining your on-premise ECC system for the foreseeable future, these commitments give you more breathing room and choice.

References

- SAP Official Announcement
(https://news.sap.com/2026/07/sap-welcomes-european-commission-decision-conclu
ding-investigation-on-premise-maintenance-support-policies/)
- SAP's Detailed Flexibility Blog
(https://news.sap.com/2026/07/evolving-maintenance-support-practices-greater-f
lexibility-sap-customers/)

entry.title: Re: RAP managed early internal numbering and static factory action - retrieve automatic guid
entry.content: This might be the solution that you are looking for if I understood the requirement correctly. So you need to do a MODIFY ENTITIES CREATE first within the action, read the MAPPED entities to get the GUID, and do another MODIFY ENTITIES UPDATE. I haven't tried it though, so not sure if the system will allow you to do both inside the factory action implementation. If by chance it didn't work, then maybe you can modify your RAP BO BDEF to have an unmanaged save option where you can update the child entities XML content data with the GUID? Or you can also try to have a determination on create implementation and see if you can read the GUID from there and update the child entity? So far, these are the options that I can think of. You can do a trial and error and see which one works and the cleanest. Please let us know which worked so we can apply the same in the future if we ever encounter this requirement.

entry.title: Re: Best Practice to Activate Registration for Indirect T...
entry.content:
Hi JuliaLu,

One scoping question first: are the DE/AT/IT transactions posted in EUR (with an EUR company-code currency), or in a foreign document currency (e.g. USD/CNY) that must then be converted to EUR for the EU return? Almost every currency-conversion restriction you listed only bites when the document currency ≠ the EUR reporting currency, so the risk profile differs a lot between the two setups.

Short answer: Yes — you can activate RITA for Germany, Austria and Italy and keep DRC cloud edition accurate, but the currency-conversion restrictions you quoted are real, documented, and today not fully removable by configuration; they only produce wrong reporting-currency amounts in specific foreign-currency constellations, and DRC gives you tooling to detect and correct the affected documents before filing.

Are DE, AT and IT supported for RITA in Public Cloud?

Yes. All three are on SAP's list of RITA-enabled countries, so no full country/region activation is needed for the tax scope. Reference: Countries/Regions Where RITA Is Enabled. Activation is SAP-guided (run the migration in SSCUI 103464, then open an incident on FI-GL-GL-F-2CL). Verify: test the migration in your Q system first and undo it if the migrated data is inconsistent. Reference: RITA in 2302 – A Pragmatic Approach.

Will RITA affect DRC / EC Sales List reporting-currency accuracy?

Yes, with limits. The behaviours you listed come from SAP's own documentation, Information on Currency Translation Where RITA Is Active, and the master restriction note SAP Note 2907372 (login required). They govern how the tax amount/base is converted into the tax reporting currency (EUR for DE/AT/IT). Key point: the company-code currency is already EUR and if documents are posted in EUR, the transaction → reporting-currency conversion is effectively 1:1 and the "amount may be incorrect" cases are immaterial. The exposure is real mainly when you post in a non-EUR document currency.

EC Sales List: reporting-currency amount and the "tax line item base amount" parameter

Constraint: with that parameter set, currency conversion is skipped when the triangular-transaction indicator is set, or when the VAT registration number is not unique. This is documented behaviour, not a defect. Reference: Information on Currency Translation Where RITA Is Active.

Mitigations: first, make sure the correct exchange-rate type for tax is configured and maintained in SSCUI 102402 Set Exchange Rate Type for Tax — if no rate is found the system falls back to an Absolute Tax Exchange Rate in C/R Currency of zero, which is where most "wrong amount" cases originate; see Currency Conversion with Prescribed Exchange Rates. Second, before filing, use Manage Tax Items in DRC with the new (2602) "Analyze Data" option to spot excluded or anomalous documents and correct them via the integrated accounting adjustment. Reference: GRC, Trade and Tax in SAP Cloud ERP 2602.

Triangular transaction (IT → AT → DE)

In your flow the Austrian entity is the middle party applying the EU simplification, so it is AT's ZM / EC Sales List that carries the triangular indicator — the exact indicator that can suppress reporting-currency conversion under the parameter above. Review the AT triangular documents specifically in Manage Tax Items before submission. If they are invoiced in EUR, no conversion is required and the limitation is moot.

The other listed limitations (cash discount, service date, down payment, Advanced ICO, Central Finance, Spain)

I won't pretend there are clean workarounds — most are current restrictions, not something you can configure away. Honest guidance: Down payment — enforce it as a process rule: down payment and final invoice in the same document/transaction currency. Cash discount / service-rendered-date rate mismatches — these only surface with non-EUR documents, so confirm whether your DE/AT/IT flows actually use foreign currency. Advanced Intercompany / weighted-average-rate in MIRO / Central Finance historical rate / Spain — where your real processes hit these, the path is a process change or an SAP incident plus a Customer Influence request, not a hidden switch. Validate each against the customer's actual scenarios rather than the whole list. Reference: SAP Note 2907372.

Tax-declaration frequency (correction to the figures in your notes)

Verify: the German thresholds quoted (€7,500 / €1,000) are outdated. Since 1 Jan 2025 (§ 18(2) UStG, Fourth Bureaucracy Relief Act) they are: monthly if prior-year VAT > €9,000, quarterly €2,000–€9,000, annual-only ≤ €2,000; the deadline is still the 10th of the following month. Reference: Finanzamt Baden-Württemberg – VAT filing deadlines. DRC supports monthly/quarterly/annual UStVA, ELSTER, EC Sales List (ZM), Intrastat and reverse charge; AT and IT have their own thresholds and forms, also covered by DRC.

Summary

RITA for DE/AT/IT is fully supported and does not by itself corrupt DRC reporting; the currency-conversion restrictions are documented and only yield wrong reporting-currency amounts when you post in a non-EUR document currency. Confirm the currency setup first, configure the tax exchange-rate type (SSCUI 102402), keep down payment and invoice in one currency, and use Manage Tax Items → Analyze Data to validate the EC Sales List / ZM (especially the AT triangular documents) before every submission. For the remaining limitations, test them against the customer's real processes and raise incidents / Influence requests only where they genuinely apply — don't assume a workaround exists.

Hope it helps.
Best regards,
Michal

entry.title: In RAP how to filter an association via button
entry.content:
Hello All,

I do not find any answer online.  So I hope you can help me out.
I got a RAP app which contains of some CDS Views in parent child relation and some "normal" associations.
Each Entity has its own UUID and a reference to the root entity.
This root UUID is used to associate to a foreign CDS and all entries are displayed within all entities.
I now want one button which takes the UUID of the specific entity and filters the data of the association accordingly
A short coding example

define root table entity rootEntity
{
  key key  : key;
      uuid : uui;

      @ObjectModel               : {
        sort.enabled             : false,
        filter.enabled           : false
      }
      _Child : composition of exact one to many children;
     
      @ObjectModel               : {
       sort.enabled              : false,
       filter.enabled            : false
      }
      _foreign : association of exact one to many foreigns on $projection.uuid = _Activities.uuid_root_reference;
}

 Child

define table entity child
{
  key parent_key : key;
  key key        : key;
      uuid       : /uuid;

      @ObjectModel:{
        sort.enabled: false,
        filter.enabled: false
      }
      _parent : association to parent parent on $projection.parent_key = parent.key;
      
}

 on Consumption level

root

define root view entity c_parent
  provider contract transactional_query
  as projection on parent

{
  key     key   as Key,
          uuid  as UUID,
          @ObjectModel:{
             sort.enabled: false,
             filter.enabled: false
           }
          _Child : redirected to composition child c_children;

          @ObjectModel:{
            sort.enabled: false,
            filter.enabled: false
          }
          _foreign : redirected to c_foreign

}

 child

define view entity c_children
  as projection on children

  association of exact one to many c_foreigns as _foreigns on $projection.parentUUID = _foreigns.UUIDRootReference

{
  key key as Key,
  key parent_key as ParentKey,
      _parent.uuid as parentUUID,
      uuid,
         
      _parent : redirected to parent  c_parent,
      _foreigns
  
}

in parent and child bdef I now want to have this:

 action (features : instance) filterByUUID result [0..*] entity c_foreigns;
side effects {
    action filterByUUID affects entity _foreigns;
  }

The foreigns association is linked as line item reference and does not contain a filterbar.

{
        id                : 'Foreigns',
        purpose           : #STANDARD,
        position          : 10,
        label             : 'Foreigns',
        type              :  #LINEITEM_REFERENCE,
        targetElement     : '_foreigns'
      },

 In coding i want somethind like this:

READ ENTITIES OF parent IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY parent
      FIELDS ( uuid )
      WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
      RESULT DATA(lt_parents).

        LOOP AT keys ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_key>).

          LOOP AT lt_parents REFERENCE INTO DATA(lr_parent).

            SELECT
              *

            FROM
              foreigns

            WITH PRIVILEGED ACCESS

            WHERE
              uuid_root_reference = @LR_parent->uuid

            APPENDING TO TABLE
              @result.


          ENDLOOP.

      ENDLOOP.

 any hint how to achieve that I can filter the foreigns for the current entities uuid would be nice.
Thank you in advance and best regards.

Matthias

entry.title: Re: Data not coming in FinOps dashboard in BTP Build Work...
entry.content:
Manorama and team,

Hello. This may sound like a cross-subaccount type of issue from authentication and destination.
Even though you deployed the app, the runtime identity  of the user in your Work Zone subaccount does not have the map to the backend API <-- theory.

May be the issue is in the following areas:
1. Destination - Make sure the destination is pointing to the consumption API exists in the Work Zone subaccount and not just the BAS subaccount.
2. May need to create a corresponding Role Collection in the Work Zone subaccount and assign it in the users there.  The app will only fetch data if the Work Zone XSUAA instance validates the user's scope.
3. May need to make sure your Work Zone subaccount and subaccount hosting the API trust the same Identity Provider (IAS or XSUAA) otherwise, the token exchange may fail during your API call.

For more details, you may need to look at your Network HAR trace to figure out (F12 Developer tools) which of the above is the culprit.

That is just a general answer.
Other experts may shine in as well.

entry.title: Re: Collision Handling between Absences and Attendances in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking - 1H 202
entry.content:
Hello @PriyankaAgarwal ,
despite the above comments claiming there is no admin alert, we do get admin alerts for the collision resolution (Alert Type "Time Data Collision"). Maybe it's a feature newer than above comments?

The problem with these alerts now is that you can't acknowledge them. For some you might need to take action, like someone booked holidays but after all they worked, so you want to cancel or shorten the absence. For others you might not need to take action, like an overlap of few minutes working time with an absence on second half of the day. So it is good to get an alert to check the situation. But once you worked through it, you should be able to acknowledge or in any way discard the alert. Can this feature be enhanced?

Also we noticed that the warnings about collision do not disappear in some cases, neither in the time sheet UI nor in the admin alerts, even though we cleared the situation and there is no longer any overlap. We ran a recalculation of time sheet and of absences, still the collision resolution warning shows. Did you witness any such behaviour and have a tip for us?

Thank you! Anita

entry.title: Re: Turn SAP APIs into MCP Tools for AI Agents using SAP Integration Suite Free Trial
entry.content:
Hi @Arnab_G, thank you for your comment 🙂
Have you changed in both the API Artifact and in the MCP artifacts the scopes field in the Authorization components to include the "ESBMessaging.send" scope? And in both you should also be selecting the" OAuth Scope or Developer Key" auth type

Another thing that is easy to miss is the "Trust Upstream MCP Authorization" checbkox in the Authorization component of the API artifact, it needs to be enabled. Let me know if this helped!

entry.title: Re: Why we need to write more about our SAP Teaching Experiences
entry.content:
Totally agree Mateus.

I've been encouraging people at the SAP ACC APAC conference this week to do exactly this, and doing so myself.

We spend so much time creating interesting learning experiences that we often forget to document them. Yet some of the most valuable ideas I've heard this week have been simple classroom activities, small experiments, and lessons learned, not major projects.

Our community is full of creative educators. Maybe we don't need more experiences. Maybe we just need to share more of them.

So, to my SAP teaching colleagues: what's one experience sitting on your laptop, in your classroom notes, or in your memory that could help someone else? Write it down. Share it. You never know where that story might lead. 🙂

entry.title: From Prototype to Practice - or What Happens After an AI Hackathon
entry.content:
On July 14, 2026, we held the first combined alumni community session for participants of the SAP "Business meets AI" hackathons. Around 22 people joined from companies across Germany, Austria, and Switzerland. Three teams from Basler Verkehrsbetriebe (BVB), Viessmann Clean & Cold Solutions/Vexxus, and Frutura shared their experiences and where their use cases stand today. The session was set-up as a community based peer exchange. 

Where Do Hackathon Teams Stand Today?

We started with a quick Mentimeter pulse check. Most participants placed themselves somewhere between "Pilots" and "Scaling" in their AI transformation journey. When asked what happened to their hackathon prototypes, the majority answered "working on it"  somewhere between the initial POC and a productive system.

What helped most: support from the SAP coaches, a full week of focused time, getting a feeling for what is actually possible, and having a solid system landscape in place. 

The word cloud captured how people experienced the hackathon: "inspiration" came up most frequently, followed by "productive," "valuable," "enlightening," and "fun." (Actually this is a fantastic feedback :-)).

Basler Verkehrsbetriebe: AI-Supported Maintenance Orders for Life Cycle Costing

Ina Stern from Basler Verkehrs-Betriebe (BVB) works in the SAP team responsible for maintenance processes. BVB operates public transport in Basel, Switzerland, and e.g. performs life cycle costing for their bus fleet with manufacturers. Every maintenance activity needs to be billed and manufacturers have very specific requirements for how maintenance orders must be structured.

The  challenge: Creating these orders involves quiet some manual effort. Requirements are complex, mistakes can happen and rework is common. The team searched for a standard SAP solution in 2024, didn't find one, but learned about the hackathon format from an SAP contact.

The solution: The team built a conversational AI using Joule that performs similarity searches on custom tables extracted from their backend. Operators provide keywords — a component name, an error type, a specific condition and the system recommends how to structure the next maintenance order based on historical data. Because these are custom tables (not SAP standard data), the similarity search approach was essential.

The hackathon experience: BVB had no developers in-house and needed to find external support close to the deadline. They succeeded and won the technology prize at the Zurich hackathon in March 2026. Ina described the focused week as a real highlight: the team was impressed by how much they could achieve with that level of focus.

Current status of the use case: Not yet live. The two blockers are budget (the hackathon wasn't planned when last year's budget was set) and resource conflicts as the same team is currently improving another part of the process. The goal remains go-live by mid-to-end 2026.

Organizational impact: The internal visibility of the hackathon win triggered broader interest. Colleagues who were already thinking about AI felt encouraged to try. Several teams now want to participate in a future hackathon with their own use cases. As Ina put it: "We gave them some kind of push to actually go and try with AI." (I recorded a podcast with Ina in german language - check it out on Apple I Spotify)

Viessmann/Vexxus: Goods Receipt Optimization with DocumentAI and Process Automation

Oliver Gresch from Vexuxs (Viessmanns Clean & Cold Solutions internal IT partner, 100% Viessmann-owned) presented their goods receipt process optimization. The team won the WOW prize at the Munich hackathon.

The starting point: Viessmann runs SAP S/4HANA 2022, where most standard AI agents are not yet available. They came to the hackathon somewhat skeptical about what they could achieve given their infrastructure constraints.

The solution: The core process runs in SAP Build Process Automation. AI enters at two specific points: first, Document AI reads the delivery note — the paper document arriving with each parcel and kicks off the process. Second, a LLM via the AI Launchpad handles a prompt-based task using interfaces to both SAP and Google systems. The result is a mobile-friendly goods receipt process that one person can handle independently, end to end. 

Organizational resistance: The team came back enthusiastic, the organization was skeptical. Trust in SAP as an AI platform was low at the time, partly triggered by SAP's stock price decline. Google Workspace is already deeply embedded in the company, and some voices argued Google's GenAI capabilities were more proven.

The generic approach strategy: Oliver's response was to abstract the pattern: LLMs read and generate text. The workflow is deterministic. The same logic can later transition to an agent architecture. By framing it generically — "look at what LLMs do best, not at one specific use case" and by identifying similar processes across the organization, the team is gradually building internal support. (I would add: we see many use cases involving DocumentAI as one pattern to use AI for process automation).

Current status: Not yet live, but the team is now identifying and preparing multiple similar use cases for step-by-step rollout. They're also waiting for Joule Studio 2.0 to become available for the transition from BPA-based workflows to proper agent architectures.

You can listen here to the podcast with Oliver and his colleague Wolfgang Schade on Apple I Spotify.

Frutura: SAP Health Check Agent: From the Vienna Hackathon to Production in Three Months

The Futura team presented a very advanced use case and probably the fastest path from prototype to production we've seen so far. Futura is Austria's main supplier of fruits and vegetables for SPAR supermarkets, with around 1,000 employees and logistics operations running nearly 24/7.

The problem: Their SAP system produces around 400,000 SMQ2 queue entries per day. About 60-80 of those require manual attention. The issue isn't workload — it's detection time. If an error in the queue remains unresolved, ERP and Extended Warehouse Management can become inconsistent. That can lead to incorrect planning, blocked goods receipts, or failed deliveries. Because logistics doesn't stop on weekends, problems can emerge at any time.

The solution: The SAP Health Check Agent continuously monitors relevant SAP queues, automatically analyzes errors, identifies critical issues before they affect logistics, and summarizes system health in a clear overview. It reduces the need for manual checks and lets operators focus on resolving the important issues.

Technical architecture: Built as a Node.js application with the SAP Cloud SDK. The agent framework runs inside the app and connects to SAP via OData for reading SMQ2 entries. Agent-to-agent communication connects it to additional SAP tools. A technical user with restricted authorization (only custom OData functions) handles system access through Cloud Connector and SAP Integration Suite.

The path to production:

  • Before the Vienna hackathon (April 2026): concept and use case design completed
  • During the hackathon week: first working prototype built
  • After the hackathon: prototype refined, adjustments for production readiness
  • Current status: beta phase is running with manually triggered tests on the productive system
  • Next step: fully automated go-live the following week (20th of July), running as a job between 5am and 8pm

The time restriction is intended as that's when most logistics activity happens, and it's when developers can safely monitor the agent's behavior and correct mistakes during the initial phase.

Future roadmap: The team plans to connect the agent to their Confluence documentation and to SAP notes for a richer knowledge base. They're also integrating it into their internal company AI platform "Dolores" giving SAP developers a cockpit to monitor the agent and trigger it manually. Eventually, the agent should expand beyond SMQ2 to other parts of the SAP ecosystem.

What Slows Teams Down After the Hackathon?

Across all three cases, common patterns emerge:

  • Budget timing: Hackathon participation wasn't planned in the previous year's budget cycle, creating funding gaps for productionization.
  • Resource conflicts: The same people who build AI prototypes are often tied up in other improvement projects.
  • Organizational skepticism: Returning with enthusiasm doesn't automatically translate into organizational commitment — especially when platform strategy decisions are contested.
  • Technical readiness: Teams on older SAP releases (e.g., S/4HANA 2022) face limitations with embedded AI features and are waiting for new tooling like Joule Studio 2.0.

What's Next: Agent Hackathons, Public Sector, and Custom AI Services

SAP Agent Hackathon — Joule Studio 2.0 (November/December 2026)

Philipp Sütterlin introduced the next evolution of the hackathon format: building agent use cases directly on customer environments with the new Joule Studio 2.0. The format covers the full journey — from use case discovery and agent design through system setup to four days of hacking, with go-live support afterwards. Three locations, each with around 10 customer teams:

  • Frankfurt: November 10–13, 2026
  • Vienna: November 17–20, 2026
  • Munich: December 1–4, 2026

Michael Dohse from Tesa, who experienced Joule Studio 2.0 at a preview hackathon in Potsdam in May, shared his assessment: the development speed and deep SAP integration represent a significant step forward compared to the first generation of Joule Studio. If you're interested, apply early as there are technical prerequisites that SAP experts need to verify before confirming participation.

Registration: SAP Agent Hackathon Sign-up 

German Public Sector & Utilities Hackathon (November 2026)

For customers from public sector and utilities industries: a dedicated hackathon running November 9–13 in Potsdam, conducted in German language. This follows the successful Berlin edition from March 2026. Registration & Info can be found here. 

Customer-specific AI Applications (CAIA) 

For teams that need hands-on technical support beyond the hackathon, SAP's Custom AI Innovation Services offer a phased approach: scoping workshops with functional, industry, and AI experts; proof of concepts based on customer data; and full solution delivery operated by SAP to product standards. Some of the CAIA consultants already served as coaches during the hackathons, so for many alumni, these might are familiar faces. If your prototype needs development support from SAP to reach production, this is the path to explore. (Just contact your SAP Services contact or reach out to us). 

Quotes in the session 

"We (with the hackathon experience) gave them some kind of push to actually go and try with AI." Ina Stern, BVB Basel, on the organizational ripple effect

"The trust to SAP was not extremely high at that time. We had to convince people that SAP, with its tools, is able to bring value into process improvement." Oliver Gresch, Vexxus/Viessmann

"We plan to start next week with the fully automated release of our solution." — Frutura team, three months after the hackathon

"After seeing Joule Studio 2.0, I'm totally convinced that this will be the next big thing." Michael Dohse, Tesa

"A community is not an audience. Lets meet, discuss and share aexperiences." Guiding principle of the alumni session 

Practical Tips for Moving from Prototype to Production

  • Describe the use case generically: If others in your organization can see the pattern applies to more than one process, buy-in increases. Oliver's approach at Viessmann is a good example for this.
  • Secure budget proactively: Plan hackathon follow-up costs (innovation/ automation budget) in the next budget cycle if possible. Ina's experience at BVB shows this. And we know that this budget is important, but not always easy to get, esp. during the year if not budgeted.  
  • Start with a controlled scope: Frutura's approach of limiting automated agent operation to specific hours depending on possible human oversightht reduces risk and builds trust with stakeholders. Its also really pragmatic. 
  • Use the hackathon for internal communication: The visibility and awards create momentum. Share results broadly, as it pushes people who were already considering AI to actually start. And it shows the possibilities of AI which are so broad. 
  • Don't wait for perfect tooling: Use what's available today (Build Process Automation, Doc AI, AI Launchpad) and design your architecture so you can migrate to agent frameworks later.
  • Get support where needed: Whether it's the Agent Hackathon for hands-on Joule Studio 2.0 experience or CAIA for professional development support, use the formats SAP offers to accelerate your path.

How shall we go on?

When asked "​How often do you want to meet?" 80% said " Quarterly - so will align to this and meet back  in October.

We also asked  "What do you want to do in the next session?"  most voted for an Expert-Demo for Joule Studio 2.0, follwed by a 
Promptathon with new Tools as well as experience sharing. Sounds like a great plan for the next three sessions. Come to the Alumni LinkedIn group  to also exchange digitally - thanks to the Swiss team who opened it). 

My Take

This was the first Alumni Session I moderated and I was especially impressed by all the expertise and the open sharing of experiences as well open discussions. We also see that challenges often are organizational and human, e.g. budget cycles, resource allocation, or trust. The hackathon creates energy and motivation about what's possible and is a great format and boost for an AI Transformation. 

I am looking forward to the next quarterly session, probably with an interactive Joule Studio 2.0 demo and more experience reports from teams that made it to production. 

FAQ

Is Joule Studio 2.0 available now? Not yet for general availability. SAP is onboarding customers through the Agent Hackathon format (November/December 2026). Apply via the sign-up page to get the process started, SAP will check technical prerequisites before confirming participation.

Can teams switch from Build Process Automation to agents later? Yes. Oliver from Viessmann described their approach: since the workflow logic remains the same, you can start with deterministic BPA processes today and migrate to an agent architecture when the tooling matures. The same APIs and logic apply as agents just remove the deterministic constraint. (my take: you also do not always need agents to automate processes).

How often will this community meet? Quarterly. The group voted for this cadence. Next session likely in October 2026, with a Joule Studio 2.0 expert demo and further experience sharing on the agenda.

Mentioned Resources & Links

  • SAP Agent Hackathon — Frankfurt, Vienna, Munich (Nov/Dec 2026) | Registration & Info
  • German Public Sector & Utilities Hackathon — November 9-13, 2026 in Potsdam |  Registration & Info
  • SAP Zurich Hackathon — September 14-18, 2026 | ​Registration & Info
  • Customer-specific AI Applications (CAIA) — Custom AI development by SAP | Contact your SAP services representative
  • ​SAPNews: KI-Hackathons als Innovationsmotor: von der Idee zum MVP incl. many use case descriptions 
  • Hackathons als Innovationsmotor und Antreiber von AI Transformationen mit Alexander Finger – Apple I Spotify
  • Vom Hackathon zur Praxis: KI bei den Basler Verkehrs-Betrieben mit Ina Stern – Apple I Spotify
  • Viessmann Clean & Cold Solutions Erfahrungen beim SAP Business meets AI Hackathon – Apple I Spotify
  • Erfahrungen im SAP Hackathon zu KI im Vertrieb bei Sto – Apple I Spotify
  • LEONI Video - Use Case HR Flow AI, going live summer 2026 
  • Alumni LinkedIn Group - Cross-cohort networking, opened by the Swiss team

entry.title: From SAP Integration Suite iFlow to MCP Tools: Exposing OData APIs to AI Agents
entry.content:
What is MCP and why does it matter for SAP?

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets AI agents discover and invoke tools at runtime. Instead of hardcoding API calls into your agent, you publish an MCP Server — a runtime endpoint that advertises a list of named, described tools. The agent reads those descriptions and decides autonomously which tool to call and with which parameters.

SAP Integration Suite now acts as an enterprise MCP Gateway: it hosts MCP Servers on the Integration Cell runtime, enforces OAuth2 authentication, rate limiting and audit logging, and lets you govern exactly which agents can call which tools.

In this post I'll show you how to go from zero to a working MCP Server in SAP Integration Suite (BTP Trial) — using the Northwind OData V4 API as the backend. Northwind uses the same OData V4 protocol as SAP S/4HANA and SuccessFactors, so everything here maps directly to real SAP backends.

The full end-to-end stack looks like this:

AI Agent (Claude Code)
   ↓  OAuth2 client_credentials  (scope: ESBMessaging.send)
MCP Server  —  Integration Cell runtime
   ↓  forwards Bearer token
Cloud Integration iFlow  (Northwind_API)
   ↓  CamelHttpPath + CamelHttpQuery passthrough
Northwind OData V4   https://services.odata.org/V4/Northwind/Northwind.svc/

Prerequisites

  • SAP BTP trial account
  • SAP Integration Suite activated (trial plan) with Cloud Integration + API Management capabilities
  • Integration Cell runtime enabled and Active (Settings → Runtimes)
  • SAP Process Integration Runtime service instance — plan integration-flow — with a Service Key

Step 1 — Enable the Integration Cell runtime

Go to SAP Integration Suite → Settings → Runtime Profiles and activate both Cloud Integration and Integration Cell.

Then go to Settings → Runtimes and confirm both show status Active. The MCP Server artifact requires the Integration Cell runtime — it cannot be deployed to Cloud Integration.

Step 2 — Create an Integration Package

Go to Design → Integrations and APIs → Create and create a new package. I named mine MCP_TEST_IS. All artifacts for this PoC live here: the iFlow and the MCP Server.

 

Step 3 — Build the iFlow

Inside the package, create a new Integration Flow called Northwind_API. The flow is deliberately simple: a single HTTPS Sender → HTTP Receiver with no transformation steps.

HTTPS Sender

Field Value

Address/northwind/*
CSRF ProtectedNo
AuthorizationUser Role: ESBMessaging.send

The wildcard address /northwind/* means a single iFlow handles all three entity paths (/northwind/Employees, /northwind/Customers, /northwind/Orders). SAP CPI automatically captures the dynamic segment in the Camel header CamelHttpPath, and the full OData query string in CamelHttpQuery.

HTTP Receiver

Field Value

Addresshttps://services.odata.org/V4/Northwind/Northwind.svc/${header.CamelHttpPath}?${header.CamelHttpQuery}
Query(leave empty)
MethodGET
AuthenticationNone

Important: Put the full URL including the query string directly in the Address field. Do not use the separate Query field — it escapes $ signs and breaks all OData parameters.

 

No Content Modifier. No Groovy script. The two Camel headers are populated automatically by the runtime.

Deploy the iFlow to the Cloud Integration runtime and wait for status Started.

Step 4 — Create the OpenAPI spec

The MCP Server needs an OpenAPI 3.0.1 spec. Each operationId becomes a tool name that the AI agent can discover and call. The description fields are what the agent reads to understand when and how to use each tool — write them for the agent, not for a human developer.

Two fields in the spec are specific to SAP Integration Suite:

  • info.x-targetEndpoint — the Cloud Integration runtime URL the MCP Server will forward calls to
  • servers[0].url — the Integration Cell base URL (used by clients to reach the MCP Server itself)
{
  "openapi": "3.0.1",
  "info": {
    "title": "NorthWind API",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "x-targetEndpoint": "https://<your-tenant>.it-cpitrial05-rt.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/http"
  },
  "servers": [{ "url": "https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/northwind" }],
  "paths": {
    "/northwind/Employees": {
      "get": {
        "operationId": "getEmployees",
        "summary": "Get Employees",
        "description": "Returns employees. Supports OData: $filter (e.g. City eq 'London'), $top, $select, $orderby",
        "parameters": [
          { "name": "$filter", "in": "query", "schema": { "type": "string" } },
          { "name": "$top",    "in": "query", "schema": { "type": "integer" } },
          { "name": "$select", "in": "query", "schema": { "type": "string" } },
          { "name": "$orderby","in": "query", "schema": { "type": "string" } }
        ],
        "responses": { "200": { "description": "List of employees" } }
      }
    },
    "/northwind/Customers": { "...": "same pattern with getCustomers" },
    "/northwind/Orders":    { "...": "same pattern with getOrders + $expand" }
  }
}

Step 5 — Create the MCP Server artifact

Inside the same package, add a new artifact of type MCP Server.

Field Value

Namenorthwind_MCP
MCP Path/northwind-mcp
Source TypeHTTP Endpoint + OpenAPI Spec
URLhttps://<your-tenant>.it-cpitrial05-rt.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/http

Upload the OpenAPI spec in the spec editor. The MCP Server parses it and shows 3 tools:

Tool name Description

getEmployeesGet Employees — OData filter, top, select, orderby
getCustomersGet Customers — OData filter, top, select
getOrdersGet Orders — OData filter, top, select, expand

Step 6 — Configure Policies (critical)

Under the MCP Server policies, configure:

  • Authentication: OAuth2, pointing to your XSUAA token URL
  • Authorization: change the scope from API.invoke to ESBMessaging.send

⚠️This is the most common mistake. The default Authorization policy scope is API.invoke. But the SAP Process Integration Runtime service key (plan integration-flow) generates tokens with scope ESBMessaging.send. If you leave the default, every call returns 403 Forbidden.

Step 7 — Deploy

Deploy the MCP Server to the Integration Cell runtime. Wait for status Started.

 

Step 8 — Test with curl

Get a Bearer token:

TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST \
  "https://<your-tenant>.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=<CLIENT_ID>&client_secret=<CLIENT_SECRET>" \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['access_token'])")

List available tools:

curl -s -X POST "https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/northwind-mcp" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list","params":{}}'

Call a tool:

curl -s -X POST "https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/northwind-mcp" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"tools/call",
    "params":{
      "name":"getEmployees",
      "arguments":{"$filter":"City eq '\''Seattle'\''"}
    }
  }'

Results: Nancy Davolio, Laura Callahan. ✅

Step 9 — Connect Claude Code as an AI agent

Register the MCP Server in Claude Code:

claude mcp add northwind \
  --transport http \
  --url "https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/northwind-mcp" \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer <TOKEN>"

Now ask in natural language. A few examples from the live demo:

"Show me employees in Spain"
Claude calls getEmployees with $filter=Country eq 'Spain' → empty result. It then self-corrects, calls again with $select=EmployeeID,FirstName,LastName,City,Country to list all employees, and responds: "There are no employees in Spain. The 9 employees are in USA and UK."

"Give me more details about Robert King"
Claude calls getEmployees with $filter=EmployeeID eq 7 and presents his full profile: Sales Representative, London, hired January 1994, reports to Steven Buchanan.

 

"Show me the orders Robert has handled"
Claude calls getOrders with the employee filter and summarises: 72 orders total, 3 pending, highest freight $830.75, countries served across USA, UK, Germany, France, Brazil, Austria, Mexico, Spain and more.

 

The agent chains tool calls autonomously. When Spain returned no results it self-corrected without being asked. This is the power of intent-shaped tools with rich descriptions: the AI decides how to query, not just what to call.

Key learnings

1. Scope: ESBMessaging.send — not API.invoke

The SAP Process Integration Runtime service key (plan integration-flow) generates tokens with scope ESBMessaging.send. The default Authorization policy uses API.invoke. Always change it or you'll get 403 on every call.

2. CamelHttpPath + CamelHttpQuery — no Groovy needed

A wildcard Sender address (/northwind/*) plus ${header.CamelHttpPath} and ${header.CamelHttpQuery} in the Receiver Address is enough to route all entities dynamically through a single iFlow. No Content Modifier, no Groovy script.

3. Query string goes in the Address field

The Receiver's separate Query field escapes $ signs and breaks OData parameters. Put the full URL including ?${header.CamelHttpQuery} in the Address field.

4. Intent-shaped tools beat endpoint-shaped tools

Name tools after business operations, not HTTP endpoints. Write descriptions for the AI agent — tell it when to use the tool and what parameters make sense. The agent uses those descriptions to decide autonomously what to call and how.

5. x-targetEndpoint is the routing key

The info.x-targetEndpoint field in the OpenAPI spec is what the MCP Server uses at runtime to forward calls to the iFlow. It must point to the Cloud Integration runtime URL, not the Integration Cell URL.

What's next

In the next post I'll cover Approach B — API Proxy: same result but the MCP Server sits in front of an API Artifact (deployed to Integration Cell) instead of an iFlow. That adds a full governance layer — rate limiting, IP blocking, payload protection — without changing a line of iFlow logic. The key new concept there is Trust Upstream MCP Authorization, which lets the API Artifact accept calls forwarded by the MCP Server without re-validating the token.

entry.title: DOCUMENT_MAIN03 – Update QM Inspection Lot Status from DIR Classification (CV01N)
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I wanted to share a solution approach we recently implemented and also seek feedback on a few open points.

Business Requirement

When a Document Info Record (DIR) is created for specific document types:

  • YQ3 – DIA Release Certificate
  • Y57 – Production & Process Controls

and classification values are maintained as:

Category                     SubCategory Target User Status
00160 (Certificates) 002001 (CoT) QCQA (Internal: E0006)
00160 (Certificates) 002002 (CfV) CERT (Internal: E0008)

the corresponding QM Inspection Lot status should be updated automatically.

Main Challenge

The requirement needed the DIR Classification values during save processing.

Initially we tried:
BAPI_OBJCL_GETDETAIL
or 
CALL FUNCTION 'CTMS_DDB_HAS_VALUES_INTERNAL'
DOCUMENT_MAIN03~AFTER_SAVE
but 

the classification values were not available here in the database.
At the end
    " 'BAPI_OBJCL_GETDETAIL' Does not return values as we need buffer values - not the DB values
    " FM 'CTMS_DDB_HAS_VALUES_INTERNAL' gave no result
    " This FM only return values in BEFORE_SAVE method, not in AFTER_SAVE.
    CALL FUNCTION 'CLFM_GET_INTERNAL_TABLES'
      EXPORTING
        i_allausp    = abap_true
      TABLES
        exp_ausp_tab = lt_rmclausp.​
Works in                                                        : I F_EX_DOCUMENT_MAIN03~BEFORE_SAVE
Does not return the required values in: IF_EX_DOCUMENT_MAIN03~AFTER_SAVE

FINAL APPROACH

1. DOCUMENT_MAIN03~BEFORE_SAVE
   - Read Classification buffer values using
     CLFM_GET_INTERNAL_TABLES.
   - Determine the target QM User Status.
   - Store the status for subsequent processing.

 

2. DOCUMENT_MAIN03~AFTER_SAVE
   - Determine the linked Inspection Lot.
QALS
↓
MCH1/MCHA
↓
MARA
   - Trigger a status update through Update Task processing.
  CALL FUNCTION 'STATUS_CHANGE_EXTERN'
    EXPORTING
      objnr               = lv_objnr
      user_status         = iv_status
    EXCEPTIONS
      object_not_found    = 1
      status_inconsistent = 2
      status_not_allowed  = 3
      OTHERS              = 4.

entry.title: Assistance Required – “Request Returned No Data” Error in SAP Signavio Process Intelligence
entry.content:
Subject: Assistance Required – “Request Returned No Data” Error in SAP Signavio Process Intelligence

We are facing an issue in SAP Signavio Process Intelligence and would appreciate your assistance in resolving it.

We have completed the following steps:

  • Loaded data for all required objects using Flat Files as the source.
  • Created a Case Scope and defined the lead object as Vendor Invoice for the Accounts Payable (AP) process.

However, when we create a Process Discovery widget in Process Analysis, the widget displays the following error message:

"The request returned no data."

Could you please help us identify the root cause and suggest the necessary steps to resolve this issue?

Kindly find the attached screenshot for your reference.

Thank you for your support.
